From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Indigenous Polynesian people of New Zealand

This article is about the Māori people of New Zealand. For the Māori people of the Cook Islands, see [Cook Islanders](/wiki/Cook_Islanders "Cook Islanders"). For the Maohi people of the Society Islands, see [Tahitians](/wiki/Tahitians "Tahitians").

Not to be confused with [Murri people](/wiki/Murri_people "Murri people"), [Maouri people](/wiki/Maouri_people "Maouri people"), [Mauri](/wiki/Mauri "Mauri"), or [Moriori](/wiki/Moriori "Moriori").

**Māori**[[i]](#cite_note-14) are the [indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Oceania "Indigenous peoples of Oceania") [Polynesian people](/wiki/Polynesians "Polynesians") of mainland [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). The Māori are descended from East [Polynesian](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia") settlers who arrived in New Zealand in several waves of [canoe voyages](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_migration_canoes "Māori migration canoes") between roughly 1320 and 1350.[[14]](#cite_note-Walters_et_al_(2017)-15) Over several centuries in isolation, these settlers developed [a distinct culture](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_culture "Māori culture"), whose language, mythology, crafts, and performing arts evolved independently from those of other eastern Polynesian cultures. Some early Māori moved to the [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands"), where their descendants became New Zealand's other indigenous Polynesian ethnic group, the [Moriori](/wiki/Moriori "Moriori").[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-King-Moriori-17)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Early contact between Māori and Europeans, starting in the 18th century, ranged from beneficial trade to lethal violence; Māori actively adopted many technologies from the newcomers. With the signing of the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") in 1840, the two cultures coexisted for a generation. Rising tensions over disputed land sales led to conflict in the 1860s, and subsequent land confiscations, which Māori resisted fiercely. After the Treaty was declared a legal nullity in 1877, Māori were [forced to assimilate](/wiki/Forced_assimilation "Forced assimilation") into many aspects of [Western culture](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"). Social upheaval and epidemics of introduced disease took a devastating toll on the Māori population, which fell dramatically, but began to recover by the beginning of the 20th century. The March 2023 New Zealand census gives the number of people of Māori descent as 978,246 (19.6% of the total population), up from 18.5% in 2018.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19) Of those identifying as Māori at the 2023 census, 366,015 people (41.2%) identified as of sole Māori ethnicity while 409,401 people (46.1%) identified as of both European and Māori ethnicity.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Efforts have been made, centring on the Treaty of Waitangi, to increase the standing of Māori in wider New Zealand society and achieve [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice"). Traditional Māori culture has enjoyed a significant revival, which was further bolstered by a [Māori protest movement](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_protest_movement "Māori protest movement") that emerged in the 1960s. However, disproportionate numbers of Māori face significant economic and social obstacles, and generally have lower life expectancies and incomes than other New Zealand ethnic groups. They suffer higher levels of crime, health problems, imprisonment, poverty and educational under-achievement. A number of socio-economic initiatives have been instigated with the aim of "[closing the gaps](/wiki/Closing_the_gaps "Closing the gaps")" between Māori and other New Zealanders. Political and economic redress for historical grievances is also ongoing (see [Treaty of Waitangi claims and settlements](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi_claims_and_settlements "Treaty of Waitangi claims and settlements")).

Māori are the second-largest ethnic group in New Zealand, after European New Zealanders (commonly known by the Māori name *[Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")*). In addition, [more than 170,000 Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Australians "Māori Australians") live in Australia. The [Māori language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") is spoken to some extent by about a fifth of all Māori, representing three per cent of the total population. Māori are active in all spheres of New Zealand culture and society, with independent representation in areas such as media, politics, and sport.

## Naming and identification

In the [Māori language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"), the word *māori* means 'normal', 'natural', or 'ordinary'. In legends and oral traditions, the word distinguished ordinary mortal human beings—*tāngata māori*—from [deities](/wiki/Religion_of_M%C4%81ori_people "Religion of Māori people") and spirits (*wairua*).[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[ii]](#cite_note-22) Likewise, *wai māori* denotes 'fresh water', as opposed to [salt water](/wiki/Salt_water "Salt water"). There are [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") words in most [Polynesian languages](/wiki/Polynesian_languages "Polynesian languages"),[[iii]](#cite_note-23) all deriving from Proto-Polynesian \**ma(a)qoli*, which has the reconstructed meaning 'true, real, genuine'.[[21]](#cite_note-pollex-24)

Early visitors from Europe to New Zealand generally referred to the indigenous inhabitants as "New Zealanders" or as "natives".[[22]](#cite_note-25) The Māori used the term *Māori* to describe themselves in a pan-tribal sense.[[iv]](#cite_note-26) Māori people often use the term *[tangata whenua](/wiki/Tangata_whenua "Tangata whenua")* (literal meaning, "people of the land") to identify in a way that expresses their relationship with a particular area of land; a tribe may be the *tangata whenua* in one area, but not in another.[[23]](#cite_note-27) The term can also refer to the Māori people as a whole in relation to New Zealand (*[Aotearoa](/wiki/Aotearoa "Aotearoa")*) as a whole.

The official definition of Māori for electoral purposes has changed over time. Before 1974, the government required documented ancestry to determine the status of "a Māori person" and only those with at least 50% Māori ancestry were allowed to choose which seats they wished to vote in. The Māori Affairs Amendment Act 1974 changed this, allowing individuals to self-identify as to their cultural identity. Until 1986, the census required at least 50 per cent Māori ancestry to claim Māori affiliation. Currently, in most contexts, authorities require some documentation of ancestry or continuing cultural connection (such as acceptance by others as being of the people); however, there is no minimum ancestry requirement.[[24]](#cite_note-28)[[v]](#cite_note-30)

## History

Main article: [Māori history](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_history "Māori history")

### Origins in Polynesia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/First_human_migration_to_New_Zealand.svg/500px-First_human_migration_to_New_Zealand.svg.png)](/wiki/File:First_human_migration_to_New_Zealand.svg)

The Māori settlement of New Zealand represents an end-point of a long chain of [island-hopping](/wiki/Oceanic_dispersal "Oceanic dispersal") voyages in the [South Pacific](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania").

No credible evidence exists of [pre-Māori settlement of New Zealand](/wiki/Pre-M%C4%81ori_settlement_of_New_Zealand "Pre-Māori settlement of New Zealand"); on the other hand, compelling evidence from archaeology, linguistics, and physical anthropology indicates that the first settlers migrated from [Polynesia](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia") and became the Māori.[[14]](#cite_note-Walters_et_al_(2017)-15)[[26]](#cite_note-31) Evidence indicates that their ancestry (as part of the larger group of [Austronesian peoples](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples")) stretches back 5,000 years, to the [indigenous peoples of Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwanese_indigenous_peoples "Taiwanese indigenous peoples"). Polynesian people settled a large area encompassing [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga"), [Samoa](/wiki/Samoa "Samoa"), [Tahiti](/wiki/Tahiti "Tahiti"), [Hawaiʻi](/wiki/Hawaiian_Islands "Hawaiian Islands"), [Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island") (Rapa Nui) – and finally New Zealand.[[27]](#cite_note-Wilmshurst_et_al-32)

The date of first arrival and settlement is a matter of debate.[[28]](#cite_note-Wilson2020-33) There may have been some exploration and settlement before the eruption of [Mount Tarawera](/wiki/Mount_Tarawera "Mount Tarawera") (c. 1315), based on finds of bones from Polynesian rats and rat-gnawed shells,[[29]](#cite_note-34) and evidence of widespread forest fires in the decade or so prior.[[30]](#cite_note-35)[[31]](#cite_note-36) One 2022 study using advanced [radiocarbon technology](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") suggests that "early Māori settlement happened in the North Island between AD 1250 and AD 1275".[[32]](#cite_note-37)[[33]](#cite_note-38) However, a synthesis of archaeological and genetic evidence concludes that, whether or not some settlers arrived before the Tarawera eruption, the main settlement period was in the decades after it, somewhere between 1320 and 1350.[[14]](#cite_note-Walters_et_al_(2017)-15) This broadly aligns with analyses from Māori oral traditions, which describe the arrival of ancestors in [a number of large ocean-going canoes](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_migration_canoes "Māori migration canoes") (*[waka](/wiki/Waka_(canoe) "Waka (canoe)")*) as a planned mass migration c. 1350.[[34]](#cite_note-39)[[35]](#cite_note-40)

There is definitive archaeological evidence of brief settlement as far south as [Enderby Island](/wiki/Enderby_Island "Enderby Island"),[[36]](#cite_note-41) but there is no evidence of travel further south into Antarctic waters despite occasional speculation.[[37]](#cite_note-42)

### Early history

Further information: [Archaeology of New Zealand](/wiki/Archaeology_of_New_Zealand "Archaeology of New Zealand")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Early_Maori_objects_from_Wairau_Bar%2C_Canterbury_Museum%2C_2016-01-27.jpg/330px-Early_Maori_objects_from_Wairau_Bar%2C_Canterbury_Museum%2C_2016-01-27.jpg)](/wiki/File:Early_Maori_objects_from_Wairau_Bar,_Canterbury_Museum,_2016-01-27.jpg)

Early Archaic period artefacts from the [Wairau Bar](/wiki/Wairau_Bar "Wairau Bar") archaeological site, on display at the [Canterbury Museum](/wiki/Canterbury_Museum,_Christchurch "Canterbury Museum, Christchurch") in Christchurch

The earliest period of Māori settlement, which has been called the "Colonisation phase",[[38]](#cite_note-43) "East Polynesian" phase,[[39]](#cite_note-44) the "Archaic" period, and the "Moahunter" period,[[40]](#cite_note-45) dates from the time of arrival to c. 1500. The early Māori diet included an abundance of [moa](/wiki/Moa "Moa") and other large birds and fur seals that had never been hunted before. This Archaic period is known for its distinctive "reel necklaces",[[41]](#cite_note-NgaKakano-46) and also remarkable for the lack of weapons and fortifications typical of the later "Classic" Māori.[[42]](#cite_note-47) The best-known and most extensively studied Archaic site, at [Wairau Bar](/wiki/Wairau_Bar "Wairau Bar") in the South Island,[[43]](#cite_note-48) shows evidence of occupation from the early 13th century to the early 15th century.[[44]](#cite_note-McFadgen_and_Adds_(2018)-49) It is the only known New Zealand archaeological site containing the bones of people who were born elsewhere.[[44]](#cite_note-McFadgen_and_Adds_(2018)-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Model_Of_Maori_Pa_On_Headland.jpg/250px-Model_Of_Maori_Pa_On_Headland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Model_Of_Maori_Pa_On_Headland.jpg)

Model of a *[pā](/wiki/P%C4%81 "Pā")* (hillfort) built on a headland. *Pā* proliferated as competition and warfare increased among a growing population.

Factors that operated in the transition to the Classic period (the culture at the time of European contact) include a significantly [cooler period](/wiki/Little_Ice_Age "Little Ice Age") from 1500,[[45]](#cite_note-atholl-50) and the extinction of the [moa](/wiki/Moa#Relationship_with_humans "Moa") and of other food species.[[46]](#cite_note-51)[[47]](#cite_note-52)[[48]](#cite_note-53)[[49]](#cite_note-54)[[50]](#cite_note-55)

The Classic period is characterised by finely made *[pounamu](/wiki/Pounamu "Pounamu")* (greenstone) weapons and ornaments, elaborately carved [war canoes](/wiki/Waka_taua "Waka taua") and *[wharenui](/wiki/Wharenui "Wharenui")* (meeting houses).[[51]](#cite_note-neich-56) Māori lived in autonomous settlements in extended [hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū") groups descended from common [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") ancestors. The settlements had farmed areas and food sources for hunting, fishing and gathering. Fortified [pā](/wiki/P%C4%81 "Pā") were built at strategic locations due to occasional warfare over wrongdoings or resources; this practice varied over different locations throughout New Zealand, with more populations in the far north.[[28]](#cite_note-Wilson2020-33)[[52]](#cite_note-57)[[53]](#cite_note-58) There is a stereotype that Māori were 'natural warriors'; however, warfare and associated practices like [cannibalism](/wiki/Human_cannibalism "Human cannibalism") were not a dominant part of Māori culture.[[54]](#cite_note-59)[[55]](#cite_note-60)[[56]](#cite_note-61)

Around the 15th century,[[57]](#cite_note-62) a group of Māori migrated east to the [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") and developed into a people known as the [Moriori](/wiki/Moriori "Moriori"),[[58]](#cite_note-63) with [pacifism](/wiki/Pacifism "Pacifism") a key part of their culture.[[16]](#cite_note-King-Moriori-17)

### Contact with Europeans

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Gilsemans_1642.jpg/330px-Gilsemans_1642.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gilsemans_1642.jpg)

A drawing from [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman")'s [travel journal](/wiki/Travel_journal "Travel journal") of the first encounter between Europeans and Māori, in 1642

The first European explorers of New Zealand were [Abel Tasman](/wiki/Abel_Tasman "Abel Tasman"), who arrived in 1642, Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook"), in 1769, and [Marion du Fresne](/wiki/Marc-Joseph_Marion_du_Fresne "Marc-Joseph Marion du Fresne") in 1772. Initial contact between Māori and Europeans proved problematic and sometimes fatal, with Tasman having four of his men killed and probably killing at least one Māori, without ever landing.[[59]](#cite_note-64) Cook's men shot at least eight Māori within three days of his first landing,[[60]](#cite_note-65)[[61]](#cite_note-66) although he later had good relations with Māori. Three years later, after a promising start, du Fresne and 26 men of his crew were killed. From the 1780s, Māori also increasingly encountered European and American [sealers](/wiki/Seal_hunting "Seal hunting"), [whalers](/wiki/Whaling "Whaling") and Christian [missionaries](/wiki/Missionaries "Missionaries"). Relations were mostly peaceful, although marred by several further violent incidents, the worst of which was the [*Boyd* massacre](/wiki/Boyd_massacre "Boyd massacre") in 1807 and subsequent revenge attacks.[[62]](#cite_note-67)

[European settlement in New Zealand](/wiki/European_settlers_in_New_Zealand "European settlers in New Zealand") began in the early 19th century, leading to an extensive sharing of culture and ideas. Many Māori valued Europeans, whom they called "*[Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")*", as a means to acquire Western knowledge and technology. Māori quickly adopted writing as a means of sharing ideas, and many of their oral stories and poems were converted to the written form.[[63]](#cite_note-68) The introduction of the [potato](/wiki/Potato "Potato") revolutionised agriculture, and the acquisition of [muskets](/wiki/Musket "Musket")[[64]](#cite_note-Old_New_Zealand-69) by Māori *iwi* led to a period of particularly bloody [intertribal warfare](/wiki/Endemic_warfare "Endemic warfare") known as the [Musket Wars](/wiki/Musket_Wars "Musket Wars"), in which many groups were decimated and others driven from their traditional territory.[[65]](#cite_note-70) The pacifist [Moriori](/wiki/Moriori "Moriori") in the Chatham Islands similarly suffered massacre and subjugation in an invasion by some Taranaki *iwi*.[[66]](#cite_note-71) At the same time, the Māori suffered high mortality rates from Eurasian infectious diseases, such as [influenza](/wiki/Influenza "Influenza"), [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox") and [measles](/wiki/Measles "Measles"), which killed an estimated 10 to 50 per cent of Māori.[[67]](#cite_note-72)[[68]](#cite_note-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Reconstruction_of_the_Signing_of_the_Treaty_of_Waitangi%2C_Marcus_King_%2816044258961%29.jpg/250px-Reconstruction_of_the_Signing_of_the_Treaty_of_Waitangi%2C_Marcus_King_%2816044258961%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reconstruction_of_the_Signing_of_the_Treaty_of_Waitangi,_Marcus_King_(16044258961).jpg)

Depiction of the signing of the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") in 1840, bringing New Zealand and the Māori into the British Empire

By 1839, estimates placed the number of Europeans living in New Zealand as high as 2,000,[[69]](#cite_note-74)
and the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") acceded to repeated requests from missionaries and some Māori chiefs (*[rangatira](/wiki/Rangatira "Rangatira")*) to intervene. The British government sent Royal Navy Captain [William Hobson](/wiki/William_Hobson "William Hobson") to negotiate a treaty between the British Crown and the Māori, which became known as the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi"). In 1840, starting on 6 February at Waitangi and by the end of the year at about 50 other events around the country, this treaty was signed by the Crown and over 500 [rangatira](/wiki/Rangatira "Rangatira") Māori.[[70]](#cite_note-75)[[71]](#cite_note-76) The Treaty gave Māori the rights of [British subjects](/wiki/British_nationality_law "British nationality law") and guaranteed Māori property rights and tribal autonomy, in return for accepting British [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") and the annexation of New Zealand as a colony in the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").[[72]](#cite_note-77) However, disputes continue over aspects of the Treaty of Waitangi, including wording differences in the two versions (in English and Māori), as well as misunderstandings of different cultural concepts; notably, the Māori version did not cede sovereignty to the British Crown.[[73]](#cite_note-78) In [an 1877 court case](/wiki/Wi_Parata_v_Bishop_of_Wellington "Wi Parata v Bishop of Wellington") the Treaty was declared a "simple nullity" on the grounds that the signatories had been "primitive barbarians".[[74]](#cite_note-79)[[75]](#cite_note-80)

Nevertheless, relations between Māori and Pākehā (New Zealand Europeans) during the early colonial period were largely peaceful. Many Māori groups set up substantial businesses, supplying food and other products for domestic and overseas markets. When violence did break out, as in the [Wairau Affray](/wiki/Wairau_Affray "Wairau Affray"), [Flagstaff War](/wiki/Flagstaff_War "Flagstaff War"), [Hutt Valley Campaign](/wiki/Hutt_Valley_Campaign "Hutt Valley Campaign") and [Wanganui Campaign](/wiki/Wanganui_Campaign "Wanganui Campaign"), it was generally limited and concluded with a peace treaty. However, by the 1860s rising settler numbers and tensions over disputed land purchases led to the later [New Zealand wars](/wiki/New_Zealand_wars "New Zealand wars"), fought by the colonial government against numerous Māori *iwi* using local and British Imperial troops, and some allied *iwi*. These conflicts resulted in the colonial government [confiscating tracts of Māori land](/wiki/New_Zealand_land_confiscations "New Zealand land confiscations") as punishment for what were called "rebellions". Pākehā settlers would occupy the confiscated land.[[76]](#cite_note-81) Several minor conflicts arose after the wars, including the incident at [Parihaka](/wiki/Parihaka "Parihaka") in 1881 and the [Dog Tax War](/wiki/Dog_Tax_War "Dog Tax War") from 1897 to 1898. The [Native Land Court](/wiki/Native_Land_Court "Native Land Court") was established to transfer Māori land from communal ownership into individual title as a means to assimilation and to facilitate greater sales to European settlers.[[77]](#cite_note-82)

### Decline and revival

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/E_003261_E_Maoris_in_North_Africa_July_1941.jpg/250px-E_003261_E_Maoris_in_North_Africa_July_1941.jpg)](/wiki/File:E_003261_E_Maoris_in_North_Africa_July_1941.jpg)

Members of the [28th Māori Battalion](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Battalion "Māori Battalion") performing a [haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka") (dance) in 1941

By the late 19th century, a widespread belief existed amongst both [Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā") and Māori that the Māori population would cease to exist as a separate race or culture, and become assimilated into the European population.[[78]](#cite_note-83) From the late 19th to the mid-20th century various laws, policies, and practices were instituted in New Zealand society with the effect of inducing Māori to conform to Pākehā norms; notable among these are the [Tohunga Suppression Act 1907](/wiki/Tohunga_Suppression_Act_1907 "Tohunga Suppression Act 1907") and the suppression of the Māori language by schools,[[79]](#cite_note-84) often enforced with corporal punishment.[[80]](#cite_note-85) In the 1896 census, New Zealand had a Māori population of 42,113, by which time Europeans numbered more than 700,000.[[81]](#cite_note-86)

The rapid decline did not continue and the Māori population continued to recover in the 20th century. Influential Māori politicians such as [James Carroll](/wiki/James_Carroll_(New_Zealand_politician) "James Carroll (New Zealand politician)"), [Āpirana Ngata](/wiki/%C4%80pirana_Ngata "Āpirana Ngata"), [Te Rangi Hīroa](/wiki/Te_Rangi_H%C4%ABroa "Te Rangi Hīroa") and [Māui Pōmare](/wiki/M%C4%81ui_P%C5%8Dmare "Māui Pōmare") aimed to revitalise the Māori people after the devastation of the previous century. They believed the future path called for a degree of [assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation"),[[82]](#cite_note-87) with Māori adopting European practices such as [Western medicine](/wiki/Medicine#Modern "Medicine"), while also retaining traditional cultural practices. Māori also fought during both World Wars in specialised battalions (the [Māori Pioneer Battalion](/wiki/New_Zealand_(M%C4%81ori)_Pioneer_Battalion "New Zealand (Māori) Pioneer Battalion") in WWI and the [28th (Māori) Battalion](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Battalion "Māori Battalion") in WWII). Māori were also badly hit by the [1918 influenza epidemic](/wiki/1918_influenza_epidemic "1918 influenza epidemic"), with death rates for Māori being five to seven times higher than for Pākehā.[[83]](#cite_note-88)[[84]](#cite_note-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Whina_Cooper_in_Hamilton.jpg/250px-Whina_Cooper_in_Hamilton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whina_Cooper_in_Hamilton.jpg)

[Whina Cooper](/wiki/Whina_Cooper "Whina Cooper") leading the Māori Land March in 1975, seeking redress for historical grievances

Since the 1960s, Māoridom has undergone a [cultural revival](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_renaissance "Māori renaissance")[[85]](#cite_note-90) concurrent with activism for social justice and a [protest movement](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_protest_movement "Māori protest movement").[[86]](#cite_note-91) *[Kōhanga reo](/wiki/K%C5%8Dhanga_reo "Kōhanga reo")* (Māori language pre-schools) were established in 1982 to promote Māori language use and halt the decline in its use.[[87]](#cite_note-92) Two Māori language television channels broadcast content in the Māori language,[[88]](#cite_note-MāoriTVlaunch-93)[[89]](#cite_note-94) while words such as "*[kia ora](/wiki/Kia_ora "Kia ora")*" have entered widespread use in [New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English").[[90]](#cite_note-95)

Government recognition of the growing political power of Māori and political activism have led to limited redress for historic land confiscations. In 1975, the Crown set up the [Waitangi Tribunal](/wiki/Waitangi_Tribunal "Waitangi Tribunal") to investigate historical grievances,[[91]](#cite_note-96) and since the 1990s the New Zealand government has negotiated and finalised [treaty settlements](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi_claims_and_settlements "Treaty of Waitangi claims and settlements") with many *iwi* across New Zealand. By June 2008, the government had provided over NZ$900 million in settlements, much of it in the form of land deals.[[92]](#cite_note-QuarterlyReport-97) There is a growing Māori leadership who are using these settlements as an investment platform for economic development.[[93]](#cite_note-HeraldJames-98)

Despite a growing acceptance of Māori culture in wider New Zealand society, treaty settlements have generated significant controversy. Some Māori have argued that the settlements occur at a level of between one and two-and-a-half cents on the dollar of the value of the confiscated lands, and do not represent adequate redress. Conversely, some non-Māori denounce the settlements and socioeconomic initiatives as amounting to race-based preferential treatment.[[94]](#cite_note-WaitangiDebateTVNZ-99) Both of these sentiments were expressed during the [New Zealand foreshore and seabed controversy](/wiki/New_Zealand_foreshore_and_seabed_controversy "New Zealand foreshore and seabed controversy") in 2004.[[95]](#cite_note-100)[[96]](#cite_note-101)

### Māori King movement

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Tawhiao_Maori_King_1880s.jpg/250px-Tawhiao_Maori_King_1880s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tawhiao_Maori_King_1880s.jpg)

[Tāwhiao](/wiki/T%C4%81whiao "Tāwhiao"), the second Māori king, reigned from 1860 to 1894.

The [Māori King movement](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_King_movement "Māori King movement"), called the *Kīngitanga*[[vi]](#cite_note-104) in Māori, arose among some of the Māori [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") in the central North Island in the 1850s, to establish a role similar in status to that of the monarch of the British colonists, as a way of halting the alienation of Māori land.[[99]](#cite_note-nzha-105) The Māori monarch operates in a non-constitutional capacity with no legal or judicial power within the New Zealand government. Reigning monarchs retain the position of [paramount chief](/wiki/Paramount_chief "Paramount chief") of several iwi[[100]](#cite_note-Foster-106) and wield some power over these, especially within [Tainui](/wiki/Tainui "Tainui").

The current Māori monarch, [Nga wai hono i te po](/wiki/Nga_wai_hono_i_te_po "Nga wai hono i te po"), was [elected](/wiki/Elective_monarchy "Elective monarchy") in 2024.[[101]](#cite_note-p198-107) Her official residence is Tūrongo House at [Tūrangawaewae](/wiki/T%C5%ABrangawaewae "Tūrangawaewae") [marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae") in the town of [Ngāruawāhia](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ruaw%C4%81hia "Ngāruawāhia"). She is the eighth monarch since the position was created and is the continuation of a dynasty that reaches back to the inaugural king, [Pōtatau Te Wherowhero](/wiki/P%C5%8Dtatau_Te_Wherowhero "Pōtatau Te Wherowhero").

The [movement](/wiki/Political_movement "Political movement") arose among a group of central North Island iwi in the 1850s as a means of attaining Māori unity to halt the alienation of land at a time of rapid population growth by European colonists.[[99]](#cite_note-nzha-105) The movement sought to establish a monarch who could claim status similar to that of [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria") and thus allow Māori to deal with *[Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")* (Europeans) on equal footing. It took on the appearance of an alternative government with its own flag, newspaper, bank, councillors, magistrates and law enforcement. But it was viewed by the colonial government as a challenge to the supremacy of the [British monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom"), leading in turn to the 1863 [invasion of Waikato](/wiki/Invasion_of_Waikato "Invasion of Waikato"), which was partly motivated by a drive to neutralise the Kīngitanga's power and influence. Following their defeat at Ōrākau in 1864, Kīngitanga forces withdrew into the Ngāti Maniapoto tribal region of the North Island that became known as the [King Country](/wiki/King_Country "King Country").[[102]](#cite_note-walkerstruggle-108)[[103]](#cite_note-DaltonWarPolitics-109)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The Māori monarch's influence has not been as strong as it could be, partially due to the lack of affiliation to the Kīngitanga of key iwi, most notably [Tūhoe](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_T%C5%ABhoe "Ngāi Tūhoe"), [Ngāti Porou](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Porou "Ngāti Porou"), and the largest iwi of all, [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi").[[104]](#cite_note-110)

## Demographics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Maori_ethnicity_declared_in_2018.png/330px-Maori_ethnicity_declared_in_2018.png)](/wiki/File:Maori_ethnicity_declared_in_2018.png)

Māori in New Zealand in 2018

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/M%C4%81ori_New_Zealanders_population_pyramid_in_2018.svg/250px-M%C4%81ori_New_Zealanders_population_pyramid_in_2018.svg.png)](/wiki/File:M%C4%81ori_New_Zealanders_population_pyramid_in_2018.svg)

Māori New Zealanders population pyramid in 2018

Under the Māori Affairs Amendment Act 1974, a Māori is defined as "a person of the Māori race of New Zealand; and includes any descendant of such a person".[[105]](#cite_note-111) The Māori population around the late 18th century was estimated by James Cook at 100,000. Historian Michael King suggests a slightly higher figure of 110,000 is more likely.[[106]](#cite_note-112) Their numbers declined during the 19th century, to as low as 42,000; the decline has been attributed to the impact of European colonisation, including new diseases.[[107]](#cite_note-113) Thereafter the population grew rapidly.

There were 887,493 people identifying as being part of the Māori ethnic group at the [2023 New Zealand census](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_census "2023 New Zealand census"), making up 17.8% of New Zealand's population.[[108]](#cite_note-2023_census-114) This is an increase of 111,657 people (14.4%) since the [2018 census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), and an increase of 288,891 people (48.3%) since the [2013 census](/wiki/2013_New_Zealand_census "2013 New Zealand census"). The large increase between the 2013 and 2018 census was mainly due to [Statistics New Zealand](/wiki/Statistics_New_Zealand "Statistics New Zealand") starting to add ethnicity data from other sources (previous censuses, administrative data, and imputation) to the census data to reduce the number of non-responses.[[109]](#cite_note-115)

The median age of Māori was 26.8 years, compared with 38.1 years for New Zealand as a whole. 262,422 people (29.6%) were aged under 15 years, 223,860 (25.2%) were 15 to 29, 336,486 (37.9%) were 30 to 64, and 64,725 (7.3%) were 65 or older.

In terms of population distribution, 753,384 (84.9%) Māori lived in the North Island at the 2023 census and 133,656 (15.1%) lived in the South Island. Five districts had a [majority](/wiki/Majority_minority "Majority minority") Māori population: [Chatham Islands territory](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") (68.6%), [Wairoa district](/wiki/Wairoa_District "Wairoa District") (68.5%), [Ōpōtiki district](/wiki/%C5%8Cp%C5%8Dtiki_District "Ōpōtiki District") (66.2%), [Kawerau district](/wiki/Kawerau_District "Kawerau District") (63.2%) and [Gisborne district](/wiki/Gisborne_District "Gisborne District") (54.8%). The [Upper Harbour local board area](/wiki/Upper_Harbour_Local_Board "Upper Harbour Local Board") in Auckland has the lowest concentration of Māori people at 6.1%, followed by the [Devonport-Takapuna local board area](/wiki/Devonport-Takapuna "Devonport-Takapuna") (6.2%) and the [Howick local board area](/wiki/Howick_(local_board_area) "Howick (local board area)") (6.3%), The [Queenstown-Lakes District](/wiki/Queenstown-Lakes_District "Queenstown-Lakes District") had the lowest concentration of Māori outside Auckland at 6.4%.[[108]](#cite_note-2023_census-114)

Of those identifying as Māori at the 2023 census, 366,015 people (41.2%) identified as of sole Māori ethnicity while 409,401 people (46.1%) identified as of both European and Māori ethnicity, due to the high rate of intermarriage between the two ethnicities.[[110]](#cite_note-116)

The largest *iwi* by population at the 2013 census was [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi") (125,601), followed by [Ngāti Porou](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Porou "Ngāti Porou") (71,049), [Ngāi Tahu](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_Tahu "Ngāi Tahu") (54,819) and [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_(iwi) "Waikato (iwi)") (40,083). However, over 110,000 people of Māori descent could not identify their *iwi*.[[111]](#cite_note-Quickstats-117)

Outside of New Zealand, a large Māori population exists in Australia. There were 170,057 Australians identifying as Māori at the [2021 Australian census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), with 65,031 living in [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), 39,714 living in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") and 31,044 living in [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia").[[112]](#cite_note-Hamer-118) Smaller communities also exist in the United Kingdom (approx. 8,000), the United States (up to 3,500) and Canada (approx. 2,805).[[3]](#cite_note-Walrond-3)[[113]](#cite_note-uscb-119)[[114]](#cite_note-statcan-120)

## Culture

Main article: [Māori culture](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_culture "Māori culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/TamatekapuaMeetingHouse.jpg/250px-TamatekapuaMeetingHouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:TamatekapuaMeetingHouse.jpg)

*[Wharenui](/wiki/Wharenui "Wharenui")* (meeting house) at [Ōhinemutu](/wiki/Ohinemutu "Ohinemutu") village, [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") (*[tekoteko](/wiki/Tekoteko "Tekoteko")* on the top)

Māori culture forms a distinctive part of [New Zealand culture](/wiki/Culture_of_New_Zealand "Culture of New Zealand") and, due to a large diaspora and the incorporation of Māori motifs into [popular culture](/wiki/Popular_culture "Popular culture"), is found throughout the world.[[115]](#cite_note-121)[[116]](#cite_note-122) Contemporary Māori culture comprises traditional as well as 20th-century influences.

### Traditional culture

Archaeological record indicates a gradual evolution of culture.[[117]](#cite_note-123) In the course of a few centuries, the growing population led to competition for resources and an increase in warfare and an increased frequency of fortified [pā](/wiki/P%C4%81 "Pā"). Various systems also arose aimed to conserve resources; most of these, such as *[tapu](/wiki/Tapu_(Polynesian_culture) "Tapu (Polynesian culture)")* and *[rāhui](/wiki/Rahui "Rahui")*, used religious or supernatural threats to discourage people from taking species at particular seasons or from specified areas.

Warfare between tribes was common, and Māori would sometimes eat their conquered enemies or enslave them.[[118]](#cite_note-124) Performing arts such as the [haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka") developed from their Polynesian roots, as did carving and weaving. Regional dialects arose, with differences in vocabulary and in the pronunciation of some words but the language retained enough similarities to other [Eastern Polynesian languages](/wiki/Eastern_Polynesian_languages "Eastern Polynesian languages") for [Tupaia](/wiki/Tupaia_(navigator) "Tupaia (navigator)"), the [Tahitian](/wiki/Tahitians "Tahitians") navigator on [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook")'s first voyage in the region to act as an interpreter between Māori and the crew of the *[Endeavour](/wiki/HMS_Endeavour "HMS Endeavour")*.

### Belief and religion

Main articles: [Māori mythology](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_mythology "Māori mythology") and [Māori religion](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_religion "Māori religion")

Religious affiliation[[119]](#cite_note-2018_Census-125)

Traditional Māori beliefs have their origins in [Polynesian culture](/wiki/Polynesian_culture "Polynesian culture"). Concepts such as *[tapu](/wiki/Tapu_(Polynesian_culture) "Tapu (Polynesian culture)")* (sacred), *[noa](/wiki/Tapu_(Polynesian_culture)#Noa "Tapu (Polynesian culture)")* (non-sacred), *[mana](/wiki/Mana_(Oceanian_mythology) "Mana (Oceanian mythology)")* (authority/prestige) and *wairua* (spirit) governed everyday Māori living, and there are also many [Māori deities](/wiki/List_of_M%C4%81ori_deities "List of Māori deities"). Today, some Māori follow a variety of Christian faiths such as [Presbyterianism](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints"), Māori Christian groups such as [Rātana](/wiki/R%C4%81tana "Rātana") and [Ringatū](/wiki/Ringat%C5%AB "Ringatū"),[[120]](#cite_note-religion-126) and also [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") and [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") denominations.[[121]](#cite_note-127)[[122]](#cite_note-128) At the [2018 New Zealand census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census"), 7.7 per cent of Māori were affiliated with Māori religions, beliefs, and philosophies; 29.9 per cent with Christian denominations and 53.5 per cent of Māori claimed [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_New_Zealand "Irreligion in New Zealand"). Proportions of Christian and irreligious Māori are comparable with European New Zealanders.[[119]](#cite_note-2018_Census-125)

Many Māori people observe spiritual traditions such as *[tapu](/wiki/Tapu_(Polynesian_culture) "Tapu (Polynesian culture)")* and *[noa](/wiki/Tapu_(Polynesian_culture)#Noa "Tapu (Polynesian culture)")*. Certain objects, areas, or buildings are *tapu* (spiritually restricted), and must be made *noa* (unrestricted) by ceremonial action.[[123]](#cite_note-129) It is common practice, for instance, to remove one's shoes before entering a *wharenui* (meeting-house), a token of respect for the ancestors who are represented and spiritually present within the *wharenui*.[[124]](#cite_note-130) Another spiritual ritual is *hurihanga takapau* (purification), practised when fishing to ensure there is no *tapu* on the fish.[[125]](#cite_note-131)

### Performing arts

Cultural performance of *[waiata](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_music "Māori music")* (song), *[haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka")* (dance), *tauparapara* (chants) and *mōteatea* (poetry) are used by Māori to express and pass on knowledge and understanding about history, communities, and relationships.[[126]](#cite_note-132) *[Kapa haka](/wiki/Kapa_haka "Kapa haka")* is a Māori performance art[[127]](#cite_note-133) believed to have originated with the legendary figure [Tinirau](/wiki/Tinirau "Tinirau"). It was performed for tourists following European contact, starting in the 1880s; this sometimes involved adaptations to make it more familiar to European audiences.[[128]](#cite_note-V.Smith-2014-134) It was used in the First World War to raise money for the Maori Soldiers' Fund encouraged by [Āpirana Ngata](/wiki/%C4%80pirana_Ngata "Āpirana Ngata").[[128]](#cite_note-V.Smith-2014-134) A *haka* is often performed in a *[pōwhiri](/wiki/P%C5%8Dwhiri "Pōwhiri")* (welcoming ceremony).[[129]](#cite_note-Keane-135)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Te_Matatini_2015_027.jpg/250px-Te_Matatini_2015_027.jpg)](/wiki/File:Te_Matatini_2015_027.jpg)

[Te Matatini](/wiki/Te_Matatini "Te Matatini") 2015 in [Hagley Park, Christchurch](/wiki/Hagley_Park,_Christchurch "Hagley Park, Christchurch")

Since 1972, there has been a regular national *kapa haka* competition, the [Te Matatini](/wiki/Te_Matatini "Te Matatini") National Festival, organised by the Aotearoa Traditional Māori Performing Arts Society. There are *kapa haka* groups in schools, tertiary institutions, and workplaces, and it is performed at tourist venues across the country.[[130]](#cite_note-Diamond-136)[[131]](#cite_note-Swarbrick-137)

*[Whare tapere](/w/index.php?title=Whare_tapere&action=edit&redlink=1 "Whare tapere (page does not exist)")* (entertainment houses) were a site of story-telling, dance, and puppetry in pre-European Māori culture.[[132]](#cite_note-138)[[133]](#cite_note-139) Māori theatre and contemporary dance flourished in the 1970s and 1980s with groups such as [Te Ohu Whakaari](/wiki/Te_Ohu_Whakaari "Te Ohu Whakaari"), Te Ika a Maui Players and [Taki Rua](/wiki/Taki_Rua "Taki Rua"). Contemporary Māori stage writers, actors and directors include [George Henare](/wiki/George_Henare "George Henare"), [Riwia Brown](/wiki/Riwia_Brown "Riwia Brown"), [Hone Kouka](/wiki/Hone_Kouka "Hone Kouka"), [Nancy Brunning](/wiki/Nancy_Brunning "Nancy Brunning"), [Jim Moriarty](/wiki/Jim_Moriarty "Jim Moriarty"), [Briar Grace-Smith](/wiki/Briar_Grace-Smith "Briar Grace-Smith"), and many others.[[134]](#cite_note-140) Contemporary performing arts include theatre companies Taki Rua,[[135]](#cite_note-141) Tawata Productions who run an annual playwriting festival for indigenous writers called Breaking Ground,[[136]](#cite_note-142)[[137]](#cite_note-143) and dance companies, [Atamira Dance Company](/wiki/Atamira_dance_company "Atamira dance company") and Okareka Dance Company.[[138]](#cite_note-144) In [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland") there is Te Pou, a kaupapa Māori performing arts venue that develops and partners with Māori theatre makers.[[139]](#cite_note-145)

Traditional Māori instruments are [taonga pūoro](/wiki/Taonga_p%C5%ABoro "Taonga pūoro"). They fulfilled various roles including storytelling, religious traditions and also daily functions such as the beginning of a new day.[[140]](#cite_note-146) Taonga pūoro fall into two areas, melodic instruments such as the flute and rhythmic instruments such as [poi](/wiki/Poi_(performance_art) "Poi (performance art)") "balls of dried flax on string that are swung and tapped".[[141]](#cite_note-147)

Prominent Māori music artists and groups include [Stan Walker](/wiki/Stan_Walker "Stan Walker"), [Maisey Rika](/wiki/Maisey_Rika "Maisey Rika"), [Young Sid](/wiki/Young_Sid "Young Sid"), [William Singe](/wiki/William_Singe "William Singe"), [Teeks](/wiki/Teeks "Teeks"), [Ria Hall](/wiki/Ria_Hall "Ria Hall"), [Rob Ruha](/wiki/Rob_Ruha "Rob Ruha"), [Pieter T](/wiki/Pieter_T "Pieter T"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Alien Weaponry](/wiki/Alien_Weaponry "Alien Weaponry"), [Sons of Zion](/wiki/Sons_of_Zion "Sons of Zion"), [1814](/wiki/1814_(band) "1814 (band)")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and [Maimoa](/wiki/Maimoa "Maimoa").

### Literature and media

See also: [New Zealand art § Prehistoric art](/wiki/New_Zealand_art#Prehistoric_art "New Zealand art"), and [New Zealand art § Traditional Māori art](/wiki/New_Zealand_art#Traditional_Māori_art "New Zealand art")

Like other cultures, oral folklore was used by Māori to preserve their stories and beliefs through many centuries. In the 19th century, European-style literacy was brought to the Māori, which led to Māori history documentation in books, novels and later television. Māori language use began to decline in the 20th century with English as the language through which Māori literature became widespread.

Notable Māori novelists include [Patricia Grace](/wiki/Patricia_Grace "Patricia Grace"), [Witi Ihimaera](/wiki/Witi_Ihimaera "Witi Ihimaera") and [Alan Duff](/wiki/Alan_Duff "Alan Duff"). *[Once Were Warriors](/wiki/Once_Were_Warriors_(film) "Once Were Warriors (film)")*, a 1994 film adapted from a 1990 [novel of the same name](/wiki/Once_Were_Warriors "Once Were Warriors") by Alan Duff, brought the plight of some urban Māori to a wide audience. It was the highest-grossing film in New Zealand until 2006,[[142]](#cite_note-148)[[143]](#cite_note-149) and received international acclaim, winning several international film prizes.[[144]](#cite_note-150) While some Māori feared that viewers would consider the violent male characters an accurate portrayal of Māori men, most critics praised it as exposing the raw side of [domestic violence](/wiki/Domestic_violence "Domestic violence").[[145]](#cite_note-151)

Other major films with Māori themes or subjects include [*Utu*](/wiki/Utu_(film) "Utu (film)") (1983), *[The Piano](/wiki/The_Piano "The Piano")* (1993), *[Whale Rider](/wiki/Whale_Rider "Whale Rider")* (2002), *[River Queen](/wiki/River_Queen "River Queen")* (2005), *[Boy](/wiki/Boy_(2010_film) "Boy (2010 film)")* (2010), *[Hunt for the Wilderpeople](/wiki/Hunt_for_the_Wilderpeople "Hunt for the Wilderpeople")* (2016) and [*Muru*](/wiki/Muru_(film) "Muru (film)") (2022). *[The Maori Merchant of Venice](/wiki/The_Maori_Merchant_of_Venice "The Maori Merchant of Venice")* (2002) was notable as a complete Māori language translation and performance of Shakespeare's *[The Merchant of Venice](/wiki/The_Merchant_of_Venice "The Merchant of Venice")*.

Prominent Māori actors include [Temuera Morrison](/wiki/Temuera_Morrison "Temuera Morrison"), [Cliff Curtis](/wiki/Cliff_Curtis "Cliff Curtis"), [Jemaine Clement](/wiki/Jemaine_Clement "Jemaine Clement"), [Lawrence Makoare](/wiki/Lawrence_Makoare "Lawrence Makoare"), [Miriama Smith](/wiki/Miriama_Smith "Miriama Smith"), [Manu Bennett](/wiki/Manu_Bennett "Manu Bennett"), [Keisha Castle-Hughes](/wiki/Keisha_Castle-Hughes "Keisha Castle-Hughes"), [James Rolleston](/wiki/James_Rolleston "James Rolleston"), [Rena Owen](/wiki/Rena_Owen "Rena Owen"), [Shavaughn Ruakere](/wiki/Shavaughn_Ruakere "Shavaughn Ruakere") and [Julian Dennison](/wiki/Julian_Dennison "Julian Dennison"). In most cases their roles in [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood_(film_industry) "Hollywood (film industry)") productions have them portraying ethnic groups other than Māori.

In the 2010s Māori actor-director [Taika Waititi](/wiki/Taika_Waititi "Taika Waititi") rose to global fame directing the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") film *[Thor: Ragnarok](/wiki/Thor:_Ragnarok "Thor: Ragnarok")* (2017),[[146]](#cite_note-152) and the [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award")-winning *[Jojo Rabbit](/wiki/Jojo_Rabbit "Jojo Rabbit")* (2019),[[147]](#cite_note-153) in which he played [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") in a supporting role. Waititi's previous films *[Boy](/wiki/Boy_(2010_film) "Boy (2010 film)")*[[148]](#cite_note-screen-154) and *[Hunt for the Wilderpeople](/wiki/Hunt_for_the_Wilderpeople "Hunt for the Wilderpeople")*,[[149]](#cite_note-155) both feature young Māori protagonists.

### Sport

See also: [List of New Zealand Māori sportspeople](/wiki/List_of_New_Zealand_M%C4%81ori_sportspeople "List of New Zealand Māori sportspeople")

Māori participate fully in [New Zealand's sporting culture](/wiki/Sport_in_New_Zealand "Sport in New Zealand"), and are well-represented in rugby union, rugby league and netball teams at all levels. Individual Māori players are members of national sports teams, and there are dedicated Māori [rugby union](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_All_Blacks "Māori All Blacks"), [rugby league](/wiki/New_Zealand_M%C4%81ori_rugby_league_team "New Zealand Māori rugby league team") and [cricket](/wiki/New_Zealand_M%C4%81ori_cricket_team "New Zealand Māori cricket team") teams that play in international competitions.

At the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Olympics "2016 Summer Olympics") in Rio de Janeiro, 41 of the 199 competitors (20.5 per cent) were of Māori descent in the [New Zealand delegation](/wiki/New_Zealand_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "New Zealand at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), with the rugby sevens squads alone having 17 Māori competitors (out of 24). There were also three competitors of Māori descent in the [Australian delegation](/wiki/Australia_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Australia at the 2016 Summer Olympics").[[150]](#cite_note-156)

The [New Zealand national rugby union team](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_union_team "New Zealand national rugby union team") and many other [New Zealand sports people perform a](/wiki/Haka_in_sports "Haka in sports") *[haka](/wiki/Haka_in_sports "Haka in sports")*, a traditional Māori challenge, before events.[[151]](#cite_note-157)[[152]](#cite_note-158)

[Kī-o-rahi](/wiki/K%C4%AB-o-rahi "Kī-o-rahi") and [Tapawai](/wiki/Tapawai "Tapawai") are two [ball sports](/wiki/Ball_game "Ball game") of Māori origin. Kī-o-rahi received an unexpected boost when [McDonald's](/wiki/McDonald%27s "McDonald's") chose it to represent New Zealand.[[153]](#cite_note-159) [Waka ama](/wiki/Waka_ama "Waka ama") (outrigger canoeing) has also experienced a resurgence of interest in New Zealand since the 1980s.[[154]](#cite_note-160)

## Language

Main articles: [Māori language](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language") and [Māori language revival](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language_revival "Māori language revival")

[![Map of New Zealand showing the percentage of people in each census area unit who speak Māori. Areas of the North Island exhibit the highest Māori proficiency.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/TeReoMaori2013.png/250px-TeReoMaori2013.png)](/wiki/File:TeReoMaori2013.png)

The Māori language, also known as *te reo Māori* (pronounced [ˈmaːoɾi, te ˈɾeo ˈmaːoɾi]) or simply *Te Reo* ("the language"), has the status of an official language. Linguists classify it within the Eastern Polynesian languages as being closely related to [Cook Islands Māori](/wiki/Cook_Islands_M%C4%81ori "Cook Islands Māori"), [Tuamotuan](/wiki/Tuamotuan_language "Tuamotuan language") and [Tahitian](/wiki/Tahitian_language "Tahitian language"). Before European contact Māori did not have a written language and "important information such as *whakapapa* was memorised and passed down verbally through the generations".[[156]](#cite_note-162) Māori were familiar with the concept of maps and when interacting with missionaries in 1815 could draw accurate maps of their *[rohe](/wiki/Rohe "Rohe")* (*iwi* boundaries), onto paper, that were the equal of European maps. Missionaries surmised that Māori had traditionally drawn maps on sand or other natural materials.[[157]](#cite_note-163)

From about 1890, Māori [members of Parliament](/wiki/Members_of_Parliament "Members of Parliament") realised the importance of English literacy to Māori and insisted that all Māori children be taught in English.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Missionaries, who still ran many Māori schools, had been teaching exclusively in Māori but the Māori MPs insisted this should stop. However attendance at school for many Māori was intermittent.
In many areas of New Zealand, Māori lost its role as a living community language used by significant numbers of people in the [post-war](/wiki/Post-war "Post-war") years. In tandem with calls for sovereignty and for the righting of social injustices from the 1970s onwards, New Zealand schools now teach Māori culture and language as an option, and pre-school *[kohanga reo](/wiki/Language_nest "Language nest")* ("language-nests") have started, which teach *[tamariki](/wiki/Tamariki "Tamariki")* (young children) exclusively in Māori. These now[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_people&action=edit) extend right through secondary schools (*kura tuarua*). Most preschool centres teach basics such as colours, numerals and greetings in Māori songs and chants.[[158]](#cite_note-164)

[Māori Television](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Television "Māori Television"), a government-funded channel committed to broadcasting primarily in Te Reo, began in March 2004.[[88]](#cite_note-MāoriTVlaunch-93) The 1996 census reported 160,000 Māori speakers.[[159]](#cite_note-quickstats-165) At the time of the 2013 census 125,352 Māori (21.3 per cent) reported a conversational level of [proficiency](/wiki/Language_proficiency "Language proficiency").[[160]](#cite_note-166)

## Social organisation

### Historical development

Polynesian settlers in New Zealand developed a distinct society over several hundred years. Social groups were tribal, with no unified society or single Māori identity until after the arrival of Europeans. Nevertheless, common elements could be found in all Māori groups in pre-European New Zealand, including a shared Polynesian heritage, a common basic language, familial associations, traditions of warfare, and similar mythologies and religious beliefs.[[161]](#cite_note-167)

Most Māori lived in villages, which were inhabited by several *[whānau](/wiki/Wh%C4%81nau "Whānau")* (extended families) who collectively formed a *[hapū](/wiki/Hap%C5%AB "Hapū")* (clan or subtribe). Members of a *hapū* cooperated with food production, gathering resources, raising families and defence. Māori society across New Zealand was broadly stratified into three classes of people: *[rangatira](/wiki/Rangatira "Rangatira")*, chiefs and ruling families; *tūtūā*, commoners; and *mōkai*, slaves. *[Tohunga](/wiki/Tohunga "Tohunga")* also held special standing in their communities as specialists of revered arts, skills and esoteric knowledge.[[162]](#cite_note-King-p42p43-168)[[163]](#cite_note-TeAra-Taonui-169)

Shared ancestry, intermarriage and trade strengthened relationships between different groups. Many *hapū* with mutually recognised shared ancestry formed *[iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi")*, or tribes, which were the largest social unit in Māori society. *Hapū* and *iwi* often united for expeditions to gather food and resources, or in times of conflict. In contrast, warfare developed as an integral part of traditional life, as different groups competed for food and resources, settled personal disputes, and sought to increase their prestige and authority.[[162]](#cite_note-King-p42p43-168)

[![Māori whānau from Rotorua in the 1880s](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Famille_Maori_1998-1361-139.jpg/330px-Famille_Maori_1998-1361-139.jpg)](/wiki/File:Famille_Maori_1998-1361-139.jpg)

Māori whānau from [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") in the 1880s

Early European settlers introduced tools, weapons, clothing and foods to Māori across New Zealand, in exchange for resources, land and labour. Māori began selectively adopting elements of Western society during the 19th century, including European clothing and food, and later Western education, religion and architecture.[[164]](#cite_note-170) However, as the 19th century wore on, relations between European colonial settlers and different Māori groups became increasingly strained. Tensions led to widespread conflict in the 1860s, and the [confiscation of millions of acres of Māori land](/wiki/New_Zealand_land_confiscations "New Zealand land confiscations"). Significant amounts of land were also purchased by the colonial government and later through the [Native Land Court](/wiki/Native_Land_Court "Native Land Court").

#### 20th century to present

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/The_M%C4%81ori%2C_Past_and_Present_-_Sydney_Mail_%281903%29.jpg/250px-The_M%C4%81ori%2C_Past_and_Present_-_Sydney_Mail_%281903%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_M%C4%81ori,_Past_and_Present_-_Sydney_Mail_(1903).jpg)

"The Māori: Past and Present", article from *[The Sydney Mail](/wiki/The_Sydney_Mail "The Sydney Mail")*, 1903

By the start of the 20th century, a greater awareness had emerged of a unified Māori identity, particularly in comparison to Pākehā, who now overwhelmingly outnumbered the Māori as a whole. Māori and Pākehā societies remained largely separate—socially, culturally, economically and geographically—for much of the 19th and early 20th centuries.[[165]](#cite_note-171) The key reason for this was that Māori remained almost exclusively a rural population, whereas increasingly the European population was urban especially after 1900. Nevertheless, Māori groups continued to engage with the government and in legal processes to increase their standing in (and ultimately further their incorporation into) wider New Zealand society.[[166]](#cite_note-172) The main point of contact with the government were the four Māori Members of Parliament.

Many Māori [migrated to larger rural towns and cities](/wiki/Urbanisation "Urbanisation") during the Depression and post-WWII periods in search of employment, leaving rural communities depleted and disconnecting many [urban Māori](/wiki/Urban_M%C4%81ori "Urban Māori") from their traditional social controls and tribal homelands. Yet while [standards of living](/wiki/Standards_of_living "Standards of living") improved among Māori, they continued to lag behind Pākehā in areas such as health, income, skilled employment and access to higher levels of education. Māori leaders and government policymakers alike struggled to deal with [social issues](/wiki/Social_issues "Social issues") stemming from increased urban migration, including a shortage of housing and jobs, and a rise in urban crime, poverty and health problems.[[167]](#cite_note-Sorrenson_1997,_pp_339–41-173)

In regards to housing, a 1961 census revealed significant differences in the living conditions of Māori and Europeans. That year, out of all the (unshared) non-Māori private dwellings in New Zealand, 96.8 per cent had a bath or shower, 94.1 per cent a hot water service, 88.7 per cent a flush toilet, 81.6 per cent a refrigerator, and 78.6 per cent an electric washing machine. By contrast, for all (unshared) Māori private dwellings that same year, 76.8 per cent had a bath or shower, 68.9 per cent a hot water service, 55.8 per cent a refrigerator, 54.1 per cent a flush toilet, and 47 per cent an electric washing machine.[[168]](#cite_note-174)

While the arrival of Europeans had a profound impact on the Māori way of life, many aspects of traditional society have survived into the 21st century. Māori participate fully in all spheres of New Zealand culture and society, leading largely Western lifestyles while also maintaining their own cultural and social customs. The traditional social strata of *rangatira*, *tūtūā* and *mōkai* have all but disappeared from Māori society, while the roles of *tohunga* and *[kaumātua](/wiki/Kaum%C4%81tua "Kaumātua")* are still present. Traditional kinship ties are also actively maintained, and the *whānau* in particular remains an integral part of Māori life.[[169]](#cite_note-175)

### *Marae*, *hapū* and *iwi*

[![Whenuakura Marae in Taranaki](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Whenuakura_Marae%2C_Taranaki%2C_New_Zealand.jpg/250px-Whenuakura_Marae%2C_Taranaki%2C_New_Zealand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whenuakura_Marae,_Taranaki,_New_Zealand.jpg)

[Whenuakura](/wiki/Whenuakura "Whenuakura") Marae in Taranaki

Māori society at a local level is particularly visible at the *[marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae")*. Formerly the central meeting spaces in traditional villages, *marae* today usually comprise a group of buildings around an open space, that frequently host events such as weddings, funerals, church services and other large gatherings, with traditional protocol and etiquette usually observed. They also serve as the base of one or sometimes several *hapū*.[[170]](#cite_note-176)

Most Māori affiliate with one or more *iwi* (and *hapū*), based on genealogical descent (*[whakapapa](/wiki/Whakapapa "Whakapapa")*). *Iwi* vary in size, from a few hundred members to over 100,000 in the case of Ngāpuhi. Many people do not live in their traditional tribal regions as a result of urban migration (*see [Urban Māori](/wiki/Urban_M%C4%81ori "Urban Māori")*). *Iwi* are usually governed by *[rūnanga](/wiki/R%C5%ABnanga "Rūnanga")* (tribal councils or assemblies) which represent the *iwi* in consultations and negotiations with the New Zealand government.[[171]](#cite_note-177)

### Race relations

See also: [Hori (slur)](/wiki/Hori_(slur) "Hori (slur)") and [New Zealanders § Race and ethnic relations](/wiki/New_Zealanders#Race_and_ethnic_relations "New Zealanders")

[![Protest hikoi during the Foreshore and Seabed controversy in 2004](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Hikoi_011.jpg/250px-Hikoi_011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hikoi_011.jpg)

Protest *[hīkoi](/wiki/H%C4%ABkoi "Hīkoi")* during the [foreshore and seabed controversy](/wiki/New_Zealand_foreshore_and_seabed_controversy "New Zealand foreshore and seabed controversy") in 2004

[![New Zealand endorses Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous People, 2010](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/NZ_delegation_UN_Forum_on_Indigenous_Issues.jpg/330px-NZ_delegation_UN_Forum_on_Indigenous_Issues.jpg)](/wiki/File:NZ_delegation_UN_Forum_on_Indigenous_Issues.jpg)

New Zealand endorsed the United Nations [Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples](/wiki/Declaration_on_the_Rights_of_Indigenous_Peoples "Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples") in April 2010.

Māori issues are a prominent feature of race relations in New Zealand. Historically, many Pākehā viewed race relations in their country as being the "best in the world", a view that prevailed until Māori urban migration in the mid-20th century brought cultural and socioeconomic differences to wider attention.[[172]](#cite_note-178)

Māori protest movements grew significantly in the 1960s and 1970s seeking redress for past grievances, particularly in regard to land rights. Successive governments have responded by enacting [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action") programmes, funding cultural rejuvenation initiatives and negotiating tribal settlements for past breaches of the Treaty of Waitangi.[[173]](#cite_note-179) Further efforts have focused on reducing [socioeconomic disparity](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality").[[174]](#cite_note-180)

A 2007 [Department of Corrections](/wiki/Department_of_Corrections_(New_Zealand) "Department of Corrections (New Zealand)") report found that Māori are disproportionately represented in the criminal justice system: "a number of studies have shown evidence of greater likelihood, associated only with ethnicity, for Māori offenders to have police contact, be charged, lack legal representation, not be granted bail, plead guilty, be convicted, be sentenced to non-monetary penalties, and be denied release to Home Detention".[[175]](#cite_note-181) Conversely, critics denounce the scale of assistance given to Māori as amounting to preferential treatment for a select group of people based on race.[[94]](#cite_note-WaitangiDebateTVNZ-99) Both sentiments were highlighted during the [foreshore and seabed controversy](/wiki/New_Zealand_foreshore_and_seabed_controversy "New Zealand foreshore and seabed controversy") in 2004, in which the New Zealand government claimed sole ownership of the New Zealand foreshore and seabed, over the objections of Māori groups who were seeking customary title.[[176]](#cite_note-182)

## Socioeconomic issues

Māori on average have fewer assets than the rest of the population, and run greater risks of many negative economic and social outcomes. Over 50 per cent of Māori live in areas in the three highest deprivation deciles, compared with 24 per cent of the rest of the population.[[177]](#cite_note-183)

Although Māori make up 16.5 per cent of the population,[[178]](#cite_note-184) they make up 53.0 per cent of the prison population.[[179]](#cite_note-185) Māori have higher unemployment rates than other ethnic groups in New Zealand, which is believed to partially account for their over-representation in the criminal justice system; many young Māori, finding themselves unemployed, are picked up for alcohol-related behaviours or small crimes such as vandalism.[[180]](#cite_note-186) [Underemployment](/wiki/Underemployment "Underemployment") is in turn attributed to persistent [institutional racism](/wiki/Institutional_racism "Institutional racism") in New Zealand.[[181]](#cite_note-187)[[182]](#cite_note-188)

"Only 47 per cent of Māori school-leavers finish school with qualifications higher than [NCEA](/wiki/National_Certificate_of_Educational_Achievement "National Certificate of Educational Achievement") Level One; compared to 74 per cent European; 87 per cent Asian."[[183]](#cite_note-189)
Although New Zealand rates very well globally in the PISA rankings that compare national performance in reading, science and maths, "once you disaggregate the PISA scores, Pakeha students are second in the world and Māori are 34th."[[184]](#cite_note-190) At the 2018 New Zealand census, 25.3% of Māori aged 15 and over had no formal qualifications, compared to 17.1% for non-Māori New Zealanders, and only 12.5% of Māori have a bachelor's degree or higher, compared to 26.8% of non-Māori.[[185]](#cite_note-191)

Also, a 2008 study by the New Zealand Family Violence Clearinghouse showed that Māori women and children are more likely to experience domestic violence than any other ethnic group.[[186]](#cite_note-192)

### Health

In 2017–2019, life expectancy for Māori in New Zealand was 73.4 years for males and 77.1 years for females, compared to 80.9 years for non-Māori males and 84.4 years for non-Māori females, a difference of 7.5 and 7.3 years respectively.[[187]](#cite_note-193)[[188]](#cite_note-Daly-194) However, Māori have a wide range of life expectancies across regions: Māori living in [Marlborough](/wiki/Marlborough_District "Marlborough District") have the highest life expectancy at 79.9 years for males and 83.4 years for females, while Māori living in the [Gisborne District](/wiki/Gisborne_District "Gisborne District") have the lowest life expectancy at 71.2 years for males and 75.2 years for females.[[188]](#cite_note-Daly-194)

Māori suffer more health problems, including higher levels of alcohol and drug abuse, smoking and obesity. Less frequent use of healthcare services mean that late diagnosis and treatment intervention lead to higher levels of morbidity and mortality in many manageable conditions.[[189]](#cite_note-195)[[190]](#cite_note-196) Compared with non-Māori, Māori people experience higher rates of [heart disease](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease"), [strokes](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke"), most [cancers](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), [respiratory diseases](/wiki/Respiratory_disease "Respiratory disease"), [rheumatic fever](/wiki/Rheumatic_fever "Rheumatic fever"), [suicide](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide") and [self-harm](/wiki/Self-harm "Self-harm"), and [infant deaths](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality").[[191]](#cite_note-197)

In April 2021, [the government](/wiki/Sixth_Labour_Government_of_New_Zealand "Sixth Labour Government of New Zealand") announced the creation of the first dedicated Māori Health Authority "with the power to directly commission health services for Māori and to partner with Health NZ in other aspects of the health system".[[192]](#cite_note-198)

## Commerce

See also: [Cultural appropriation](/wiki/Cultural_appropriation "Cultural appropriation")

Wider commercial exposure has increased public awareness of the Māori culture, but has also resulted in several legal disputes. Between 1998 and 2006, [Ngāti Toa](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Toa "Ngāti Toa") attempted to trademark the *haka* "*[Ka Mate](/wiki/Ka_Mate "Ka Mate")*" to prevent its use by commercial organisations without their permission.[[193]](#cite_note-199) In 2001, Danish toymaker Lego [faced legal action](/wiki/Bionicle#Māori_language_controversy "Bionicle") by several Māori tribal groups opposed to them trademarking Māori words used in the Bionicle product range.[[194]](#cite_note-200)

## Political representation

Main article: [Māori politics](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_politics "Māori politics")

[![The Māori Parliament at Pāpāwai, Greytown in 1897](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Papawai1897.jpg/330px-Papawai1897.jpg)](/wiki/File:Papawai1897.jpg)

The opening of the Māori Parliament at Pāpāwai, [Greytown](/wiki/Greytown,_New_Zealand "Greytown, New Zealand") in 1897, with [Richard Seddon](/wiki/Richard_Seddon "Richard Seddon") in attendance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Tino_Rangatiratanga_Maori_sovereignty_movement_flag.svg/250px-Tino_Rangatiratanga_Maori_sovereignty_movement_flag.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tino_Rangatiratanga_Maori_sovereignty_movement_flag.svg)

The [national Māori flag](/wiki/National_M%C4%81ori_flag "National Māori flag"), also known as the *[Tino rangatiratanga](/wiki/Tino_rangatiratanga "Tino rangatiratanga")* (absolute sovereignty) flag. Designed in 1989,[[195]](#cite_note-201) it is widely used by Māori groups.

Māori have been represented to [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") in New Zealand politics since the [Declaration of the Independence of New Zealand](/wiki/Declaration_of_the_Independence_of_New_Zealand "Declaration of the Independence of New Zealand"), before the Treaty of Waitangi was signed in 1840. Being a traditionally tribal people, no one organisation ostensibly speaks for all Māori nationwide. The [Māori King Movement](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_King_Movement "Māori King Movement") (*Kīngitanga*) originated in the 1860s as an attempt by several iwi to unify under one leader; in modern times, it serves a largely ceremonial role. Another attempt at political unity was the Kotahitanga Movement, which established a separate Māori Parliament that held annual sessions from 1892 until its last sitting in 1902.[[196]](#cite_note-202)

Māori have had [reserved seats](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_electorates "Māori electorates") in the [New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament "New Zealand Parliament") since 1868.[[197]](#cite_note-seats-203) Māori received [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") with other New Zealand citizens in 1893.[[198]](#cite_note-204) Currently, Māori reserved electorates account for seven of the 120 seats in New Zealand's unicameral parliament, and consideration of and consultation with Māori have become routine requirements for councils and government organisations.[[197]](#cite_note-seats-203) The contesting of these seats was the first opportunity for many Māori to participate in New Zealand elections, although the elected Māori representatives initially struggled to assert significant influence. [Sir Āpirana Ngata](/wiki/%C4%80pirana_Ngata "Āpirana Ngata") has often been described as the foremost Māori politician to have served in Parliament in the mid-20th century.[[199]](#cite_note-205)

Debate occurs frequently as to the relevance and legitimacy of the separate [electoral roll](/wiki/Electoral_roll "Electoral roll") and the reserved seats. The National Party announced in 2008 it would abolish the seats when all historic Treaty settlements have been resolved, which it aimed to complete by 2014.[[200]](#cite_note-206) However, after the election National reached an agreement with the Māori Party not to abolish the seats until Māori give their approval.[[201]](#cite_note-207)

Several Māori political parties have formed over the years to improve the position of Māori in New Zealand society. The present [Māori Party](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Party "Māori Party"), formed in 2004, secured 1.32 per cent of the [party vote](/wiki/Party_vote "Party vote") at the [2014 general election](/wiki/2014_New_Zealand_general_election "2014 New Zealand general election") and held two seats in the [51st New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/51st_New_Zealand_Parliament "51st New Zealand Parliament"), with two MPs serving as Ministers outside [Cabinet](/wiki/New_Zealand_Cabinet "New Zealand Cabinet"). The party did not achieve any representatives in the [52nd New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/52nd_New_Zealand_Parliament "52nd New Zealand Parliament"),[[202]](#cite_note-208) but regained two seats in the [53rd](/wiki/53rd_New_Zealand_Parliament "53rd New Zealand Parliament").[[203]](#cite_note-209)

Following the 2020 reelection of the [New Zealand Labour Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party") to government, Labour Minister [Nanaia Mahuta](/wiki/Nanaia_Mahuta "Nanaia Mahuta") became the first female Māori [Foreign Minister of New Zealand](/wiki/Minister_of_Foreign_Affairs_(New_Zealand) "Minister of Foreign Affairs (New Zealand)"); she replaced [Winston Peters](/wiki/Winston_Peters "Winston Peters"), also Māori, in the role. In 2016 she became the first Member of Parliament to have *moko kauae* (the traditional Māori female facial tattoo).[[204]](#cite_note-210) In the 2020 election more MPs with *moko kauae* entered Parliament, including Māori Party co-leader [Debbie Ngarewa-Packer](/wiki/Debbie_Ngarewa-Packer "Debbie Ngarewa-Packer") and [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Aotearoa_New_Zealand "Green Party of Aotearoa New Zealand") MP [Elizabeth Kerekere](/wiki/Elizabeth_Kerekere "Elizabeth Kerekere").

The [54th Parliament](/wiki/54th_New_Zealand_Parliament "54th New Zealand Parliament") after the [2023 New Zealand general election](/wiki/2023_New_Zealand_general_election "2023 New Zealand general election") has seen a historically high number of Māori MPs at 33. Māori are at 27% of the parliament while in the general population they are 17%.[[205]](#cite_note-211)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** Māori: [[ˈmaːɔɾi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Rar-M%C4%81ori.ogg "File:Rar-Māori.ogg"); New Zealand English: [/ˈmʌːori/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") or [/ˈmæʊɹiː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); UK English: [/ˈmaʊri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Also spelled *Maori*[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) or, uncommonly, *Maaori*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-22)** *Māori* has cognates in other [Polynesian languages](/wiki/Polynesian_languages "Polynesian languages"), such as [Hawaiian](/wiki/Hawaiian_language "Hawaiian language") *maoli*, [Tahitian](/wiki/Tahitian_language "Tahitian language") *mā'ohi*, and [Cook Islands Māori](/wiki/Cook_Islands_M%C4%81ori "Cook Islands Māori") *māori*, which all share similar meanings.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-23)** E.g. *[kanaka maoli](/wiki/Kanaka_maoli "Kanaka maoli")*, meaning native [Hawaiian](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"). (In the [Hawaiian language](/wiki/Hawaiian_language "Hawaiian language"), the Polynesian letter "T" regularly becomes a "K", and the Polynesian letter "R" regularly becomes an "L".)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-26)** The orthographic conventions developed by the [Māori Language Commission](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Language_Commission "Māori Language Commission") (*Te Taura Whiri i te Reo Māori*) recommend the use of the [macron](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)") (ā ē ī ō ū) to denote long vowels. Contemporary English-language usage in New Zealand tends to avoid the [anglicised](/wiki/Anglicise "Anglicise") plural form of the word *Māori* with an "s": The Māori language generally marks plurals by changing the [article](/wiki/Article_(grammar) "Article (grammar)") rather than the noun, for example: *te waka* (the canoe); *ngā waka* (the canoes).
5. **[^](#cite_ref-30)** In 2003, [Christian Cullen](/wiki/Christian_Cullen "Christian Cullen") became a member of the [Māori rugby team](/wiki/New_Zealand_M%C4%81ori_rugby_union_team "New Zealand Māori rugby union team") despite having, according to his father, about 1/64 Māori ancestry.[[25]](#cite_note-29)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-104)** Also spelled *Kiingitanga*. The preferred [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") of the [Waikato-Tainui](/wiki/Waikato-Tainui "Waikato-Tainui") [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") is to use doubled vowels rather than [macrons](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic)#Vowel_length "Macron (diacritic)") to indicate long vowels.[[97]](#cite_note-102)[[98]](#cite_note-103)
